growth_records.medium: Minimal defined medium with glucose as sole carbon source (phosphate-buffered, pH 7)

up.method: HPLC (Aminex-87H); uptake rate = slope of [glucose] vs cell density

sec.method: HPLC; described as a minor product

mfa.note: Fig. 1 FBA flux map for C. pasteurianum; per-product relative fluxes only in figure image; absolute values in SI Appendix Table S6

growth_records.cite: Mueller, Elliott P. et al. Metabolic controls on the carbon isotope fractionations of bacterial fermentation. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America (2025). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2511040122. [PMC12541407]
growth_records.snippet: C. pasteurianum made acetate, butyrate, and a minor amount of formate. ... Growth rates for E. coli, V. fischeri, C. pasteurianum are similar at 0.47, 0.434, and 0.473 h-1.
growth_records.notes: mu=0.473 h-1 reported (OD600, SI Fig. S1). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.473 = 1.465 h. Butyrate-type clostridial fermentation of glucose; products acetate + butyrate + minor formate measured by HPLC. Strain not named in main text. Phosphate buffer pH 7. Full medium in SI Tables S1-S5 -> not stated. Absolute rates in SI Table S6; rate fields null (not invented). Growth temperature not stated in main text.

growth_records.medium: CGM (Clostridial Growth Medium) with 100 g/L crude glycerol as sole carbon source

up.method: HPLC (Fig. 2a); value derived from M150B consumption and reported ratio

growth_records.cite: Sandoval, Nicholas R et al. Whole-genome sequence of an evolved Clostridium pasteurianum strain reveals Spo0A deficiency responsible for increased butanol production and superior growth. Biotechnology for Biofuels (2015). DOI:10.1186/s13068-015-0408-7. [PMC4690370]
growth_records.snippet: In simple batch cultures without pH control and feeding or medium optimization, Cpa strain M150B showed a decreased lag phase, 65 % higher glycerol consumption (40.4 g/L), and 91 % higher butanol production (final titer 7.1 g/L) compared to
growth_records.notes: WT crude-glycerol condition. Only titers (g/L), no time-normalized rates or numeric growth rate given (specific growth rate shown only in Fig. 1 as function of crude-glycerol concentration). WT glycerol consumption derived: M150B 40.4 g/L = 65% higher than WT => WT = 40.4/1.65 = 24.48 g/L. WT butanol derived: M150B 7.1 g/L = 91% higher => WT = 7.1/1.91 = 3.72 g/L. WT PDO 2.6 g/L stated directly. Ethanol/lactate <0.5 g/L both strains both conditions. Acetate a CGM component (~1.8 g/L) partially consumed (uptake). Temp 37C, pH 6.8, anaerobic from Methods CGM description.

growth_records.strain: M150B (chemically mutagenized, directed-evolution-selected, asporogenous; spo0A 24-bp deletion)
growth_records.medium: CGM (Clostridial Growth Medium) with 100 g/L crude glycerol as sole carbon source
growth_records.cite: Sandoval, Nicholas R et al. Whole-genome sequence of an evolved Clostridium pasteurianum strain reveals Spo0A deficiency responsible for increased butanol production and superior growth. Biotechnology for Biofuels (2015). DOI:10.1186/s13068-015-0408-7. [PMC4690370]
growth_records.snippet: Cpa strain M150B showed a decreased lag phase, 65 % higher glycerol consumption (40.4 g/L), and 91 % higher butanol production (final titer 7.1 g/L) compared to the WT in 100 g/L crude glycerol. The WT strain produced more PDO than the muta
growth_records.notes: M150B crude-glycerol condition. Directly reported titers: glycerol consumed 40.4 g/L, butanol 7.1 g/L, PDO 1.5 g/L. Titers only, no time-normalized rate or numeric growth rate (specific growth rate qualitatively 'increased'; shown only in figures). Ethanol/lactate <0.5 g/L. Acetate partially consumed. Conditions (37C, pH 6.8, anaerobic batch) from Methods CGM description.

growth_records.medium: CGM (Clostridial Growth Medium) with 60 g/L pure (molecular-biology-grade) glycerol as sole carbon source

up.method: HPLC (Fig. 2b); derived from M150B value and reported ratio

growth_records.cite: Sandoval, Nicholas R et al. Whole-genome sequence of an evolved Clostridium pasteurianum strain reveals Spo0A deficiency responsible for increased butanol production and superior growth. Biotechnology for Biofuels (2015). DOI:10.1186/s13068-015-0408-7. [PMC4690370]
growth_records.snippet: While there was no lag phase for either the mutant or WT when grown on 60 g/L pure glycerol, M150B consumed 75 % more glycerol (43 g/L) and produced 80 % more butanol (11.7 g/L). As under the crude glycerol condition, the WT produced more P
growth_records.notes: WT pure-glycerol condition. Titers only. WT glycerol consumption derived: M150B 43 g/L = 75% higher => WT = 43/1.75 = 24.57 g/L. WT butanol derived: M150B 11.7 g/L = 80% higher => WT = 11.7/1.80 = 6.50 g/L. WT PDO 1.5 g/L stated directly (mutant 0.9). No time-normalized rate or numeric growth rate. Conditions from Methods CGM description.

growth_records.medium: CGM (Clostridial Growth Medium) with 60 g/L pure (molecular-biology-grade) glycerol as sole carbon source
growth_records.cite: Sandoval, Nicholas R et al. Whole-genome sequence of an evolved Clostridium pasteurianum strain reveals Spo0A deficiency responsible for increased butanol production and superior growth. Biotechnology for Biofuels (2015). DOI:10.1186/s13068-015-0408-7. [PMC4690370]
growth_records.snippet: M150B consumed 75 % more glycerol (43 g/L) and produced 80 % more butanol (11.7 g/L). As under the crude glycerol condition, the WT produced more PDO than the mutant from pure glycerol, but for both strains, less was produced overall (1.5 a
growth_records.notes: M150B pure-glycerol condition. Directly reported titers: glycerol consumed 43 g/L, butanol 11.7 g/L, PDO 0.9 g/L. Titers only, no time-normalized rate or numeric growth rate (growth 'superior' but specific growth rate shown only in figures). Ethanol/lactate <0.5 g/L. Acetate partially consumed. Conditions from Methods CGM description. NOTE: paper is primarily a genome-sequencing/mutation study; the only quantitative physiology is these batch endpoint titers.
